explanation.text: Kareem and Norbert research their ethnic origins, trade facts about Filipino and African achievements, and visit the Metropolitan Museum to learn more. These activities reveal Kareem's desire to understand his heritage.

Kareem contrasts his wish to satisfy adults with his wish to be like his peers and earn their friendship. That tension directly shows how classmates' opinions and acceptance can strongly affect a teenager.
Norbert is the one friend Kareem trusts with any thought or feeling because he knows Norbert will not ridicule him. This emotional safety best explains why the friendship is especially important.

Kareem describes his play as politely passive while successful players compete roughly and use their bodies with authority. Saying he lacks an aggressive nature means he knows he must change his playing style and become tougher.

Coach Donahue acts businesslike but lets a slight grin reveal that he enjoys Kareem's stunned reaction to the varsity uniform. Kareem notices the humor, which shows he is beginning to understand and appreciate the coach's personality.

Basketball gives Kareem an escape from both public unrest and his own inner turmoil, even though mastering the sport requires hard work. That relationship is central because the article shows basketball shaping how he copes and grows.

The authors show Kareem and Norbert playing chess, discussing their futures, listening to jazz, researching their cultures, and visiting a museum. These varied shared interests are the main way their friendship is developed.

The speaker searches her heart and identifies the source of her sorrow: city life has exhausted her, while she longs for the sea's wind, spray, and surf. This self-examination shows that she understands why she feels discontented.

The first stanza describes the speaker's present weariness in the city and desire for the sea. The second creates a contrast because it recalls past coastal freedom while emphasizing her current sense of being trapped beneath buildings.

Each "once again" introduces another sensory detail the speaker yearns to experience, including the harbor sights, gulls, tide, and fog bell. The repetition emphasizes her longing because every remembered sensation points toward returning to the sea.

The opening lines state both the speaker's sorrow and its cause: she is tired of the city and longs for the sea. They best support the theme because the rest of the poem expands that homesickness through coastal memories.

The speaker says she has been away from the water too long and immediately adds that she needs water nearby. This phrase states the central problem that explains her unhappiness and longing throughout the poem.

When the mother is upset or stressed, she turns to Haydn at the piano for comfort, although her busy present life leaves little time to play. This history develops the theme by showing music's lasting emotional value in her life.

The opening focuses on family food and life at Ahimsa House, but "When Josh was five, he discovered the cello" shifts the story toward his relationship with music. The direction changes because that discovery prepares for the cello's role in the ending.

Asking Josh to play cooking music brightens him after entering the empty house and leads him to perform from memory. The invitation helps resolve the sadness because his cello becomes a way to express feelings he cannot put into words.
Josh droops when he enters the empty house but brightens as soon as the narrator suggests playing his cello. This contrast shows that "wilted" describes sadness rather than tiredness or confusion.

At Ahimsa House, everyone participates by taking a turn preparing meals for the group. That shared responsibility best supports the idea that food can create community because cooking brings all members into a common activity.
Josh does not speak aloud while he plays, yet the narrator understands the music as feelings he cannot express in words. The quotation marks show that he uses the cello as his voice.

The narrator recalls Ahimsa House, her mother's piano playing, Josh's first encounter with a cello, and her father's old request for cooking music. These memories develop her point of view because they explain why she knows music can comfort Josh now.

Liz and the rest of her family dismiss the Lincoln story because they do not believe the grandfather's claim is true. In this context, "family myth" means a family fantasy or tale assumed to be invented.
The grandfather says Grace gives their ordinary family a "footnote in history" through her exchange with Lincoln. The expression means a small but meaningful part in a much larger historical story.

The blunt statement that no family member believed the grandfather establishes their certainty that his story was false. It foreshadows surprise because the displayed letters later prove that he had told the truth.

Liz's grandfather encourages her political ambitions by using Grace as proof that one person can make a difference. Paragraph 10 shows the effect of that influence through Liz's political-science degree, campaign work, and congressional job.

The fact that some brothers already planned to vote for Lincoln says nothing about how a beard would help him. The other statements support Grace's argument by claiming whiskers would improve his appearance or attract additional votes.

After Lincoln calls Grace forward and kisses her cheek, she runs away while still carrying the flowers she meant to give him. Her disorganized reaction shows that the unexpected personal attention made her nervous and overwhelmed.

The article centers on Liz's discovery that a family story she had dismissed as myth was documented history. The opening quotation best reveals that purpose because it establishes the disbelief that the Lincoln letters later overturn.
